questions.question: What is the optimal window for sending reactivation emails?

options.text: Days 1-3 of inactivity
Days 7-14 of inactivity
Days 21-30 of inactivity
After cancellation

questions.explanation: Days 7-14 is the golden window. Before Day 7 might be normal life. After Day 21, mental checkout has already happened and success rates drop below 10%.

questions.question: What percentage of churning customers give no verbal warning before cancelling?
questions.explanation: 70% of churning customers never complain or contact support — they just stop using the product. This is why proactive monitoring is essential.

questions.question: What is the typical pause-to-reactivation rate?
questions.explanation: 60-70% of customers who pause instead of cancelling eventually reactivate, compared to only 5-15% of cancelled customers who return.

questions.question: A customer responds to your reactivation email saying 'I got busy.' What's the best response?

options.text: No problem! Let me know when you're ready.
Would you like to pause for 30-60 days instead of paying for something you can't use?
Here's a tutorial to help you get more value faster.
Can I offer you a discount to stay?

questions.explanation: Offering pause is the best response. It acknowledges their situation, stops billing, and keeps the door open for reactivation when they're ready.

questions.question: What is the typical win-back rate for churned customers within 90 days?
questions.explanation: 10-20% of churned customers return within 90 days if you maintain contact through a win-back sequence. This is significant revenue recovery.

questions.question: For a customer paying $200+/month who goes dormant, what's the recommended approach?

options.text: Send them through the automated sequence
Wait until they cancel, then reach out
Send a personal email from the founder immediately
Remove them from all communications

questions.explanation: High-value accounts ($200+/mo) deserve personal attention. Send a personal email from the founder starting at Day 7-10, and escalate to a phone call if no response.

questions.question: What should Email 3 (Day 21) of your reactivation sequence include?

options.text: A discount offer to encourage them to stay
Three clear options: keep using, pause, or cancel
A list of new features they've missed
A survey asking why they stopped using the product

questions.explanation: Email 3 should give three clear paths forward: keep using (with help), pause (temporary break), or cancel (graceful exit). This makes it easy to respond and shows respect for their decision.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of Email 1 (Day 7-10) in the reactivation sequence?

options.text: Convince them to upgrade their plan
Remind them you exist without making them feel guilty
Ask them to refer other customers
Offer a discount to prevent churn

questions.explanation: Email 1 is a gentle nudge. The goal is to remind them you exist, open the door for conversation, and make it easy to respond — without pressure or guilt.
